mapQuestions.question: Which continent is the Netherlands on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, on the western edge. The Netherlands faces the North Sea, with Germany to the east and Belgium to the south 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of the Netherlands?
mapQuestions.answer: Amsterdam — though the government is actually based in The Hague. The country has a "constitutional" capital (Amsterdam) and a separate "seat of government" (The Hague).

mapQuestions.question: How much of the Netherlands is below sea level?
mapQuestions.answer: About 26%. Some parts are 6 metres below sea level. The country is kept dry by dykes (walls), canals and pumps. There is a Dutch saying: "God created the world, but the Dutch created the Netherlands."

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest point in the Netherlands?
mapQuestions.answer: The Vaalserberg, only 322 metres. It is so flat that the country has one of the busiest bicycle cultures in the world.

mapQuestions.question: Why is the Dutch national colour orange and not red/white/blue?
mapQuestions.answer: It comes from the royal family, the House of Orange-Nassau. The flag was orange-white-blue originally but the orange faded badly, so it was changed to red. Orange is still the colour for football, royal birthdays and Kings' Day.

foods.name: Stroopwafel
foods.description: Two thin wafers with a layer of soft caramel-syrup between them. Place one on top of your hot tea or coffee — the steam softens the syrup until it oozes.

foods.name: Bitterballen
foods.description: Crunchy deep-fried balls of thick beef ragout, served piping hot with mustard. Pub food, paired with a cold beer. Dangerous: the inside stays scorching for ages.

foods.name: Patatje oorlog
foods.description: Literally "war chips" — a cone of chips loaded with mayonnaise, Indonesian-style satay (peanut) sauce, and chopped raw onions on top. Messy and brilliant.

foods.name: Haring (raw herring)
foods.description: A whole raw, lightly-cured fish, eaten by holding the tail above your mouth and biting from the bottom up. Usually with chopped onion and pickles on the side.

foods.name: Poffertjes
foods.description: Small, fluffy puffed pancakes the size of a coin, served warm on a wooden plate, dusted with powdered sugar and a knob of butter melting on top.

foods.name: Dutch cheese
foods.description: Gouda, Edam, Leerdammer, Beemster — the Netherlands is the world's biggest cheese exporter. The huge wheels of cheese at the Alkmaar cheese market are still traded by men in coloured hats and white trousers.

scavengerItems.label: A bicycle propped against a wall, leaning against a canal railing, or in a huge bike park
A working windmill (still grinding flour or pumping water)
A tulip (best in April–May), or a flower-bulb field
A skinny tall canal house leaning slightly forwards or sideways
A houseboat moored on an Amsterdam canal
A pair of wooden klompen (clogs) in a souvenir shop or worn by a farmer
Someone wearing orange (the national colour, not the flag colour)
A cheese shop with giant yellow wheels of Gouda or Edam
A polder — a perfectly flat field below sea level, drained by a windmill
A book reference to Anne Frank, in a museum window or shop
A Van Gogh, Rembrandt or Vermeer reproduction in a shop window

geoMatches.description: The capital. Built on 90 islands connected by 165 canals and 1,500 bridges. Skinny tall houses, Anne Frank House, Van Gogh Museum, more bicycles than people.
The seat of government, the royal family, and the International Criminal Court. Quieter and grander than Amsterdam, with a long sandy North Sea beach 5 minutes away.
A modern port city. Mostly destroyed by bombs in 1940, it rebuilt with daring modern architecture — bright yellow cube houses, indoor markets, futuristic bridges.
The largest flower garden in the world, near the coast. Open mid-March to mid-May, with 7 million tulips, daffodils and hyacinths in massive coloured stripes.
The biggest of the Wadden Islands in the north. Wide North Sea beaches, seal colonies, sheep, and the world's largest tidal mudflat just offshore.
The oldest city in the Netherlands, in the very south. Belgian-French feel, cobbled squares, big cathedral. The 1992 Treaty of Maastricht here created the European Union.

senses.smell: Fresh stroopwafels off a hot iron, canal water, ripe cheese aging in a shop, sea air at the coast, tulips and hyacinths in April, rain on cobblestones…
senses.hear: Bike bells everywhere, "Hallo!" "Doei!", windmill blades creaking, seagulls over the canals, Dutch (a chewy mix of German and English-sounding sounds), tram bells…
senses.taste: Sweet caramel syrup oozing out of stroopwafel, salty raw herring with chopped onions, sharp aged Gouda, fluffy poffertjes dusted with sugar, dark salty drop (Dutch liquorice)…
senses.feel: Cold drizzly wind, soft tulip petals, smooth canal-stone bridges, hard bike saddles, the gentle rocking of a houseboat, fresh North Sea spray on the face…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How is most of the Netherlands below sea level?
stories.body: About 26% of the Netherlands is below the level of the sea, and some parts of the country sit 6 metres below it. Without dykes (walls) and constant pumping, much of the country would simply flood. The Dutch have been fighting the sea for over 1,000 years. They have built dykes, dug canals, used windmills (and later electric pumps), and have even taken land FROM the sea — the entire province of Flevoland was sea bottom until the 1950s. The country's most famous saying: "God created the world, but the Dutch created the Netherlands."
stories.thinkingQuestion: If most of your country could vanish under water without dykes, would you trust the dykes to keep doing their job? Or build differently?

stories.question: Why are there more bicycles than people?
stories.body: There are about 23 million bicycles in the Netherlands, more than people (17 million). Children learn to bike before they can walk properly. Parents bike to work with two kids in a front cargo box. Grandparents bike to the supermarket. Almost every Dutch city has separate bike lanes, dedicated bike traffic lights, and quiet bike-only streets. Amsterdam has more bicycles than cars. The Dutch invented the modern "city bike" with upright seating, full mudguards and a chain guard. The country is so flat that cycling is easy and so safe that helmets are almost never worn.

stories.question: What was "tulip mania"?
stories.body: Tulips came to the Netherlands from Turkey in the 1500s and became wildly fashionable. In 1636–37, "tulip mania" struck. Dutch investors traded tulip bulbs at insane prices — at the peak, a single rare bulb could cost the same as a townhouse in Amsterdam. Then in February 1637 the bubble burst overnight. Many people who thought they were rich woke up with worthless bulbs. It is sometimes called the world's first recorded financial bubble. Today the Netherlands is still the world's biggest tulip producer, exporting 2 billion tulip bulbs every year. The Keukenhof gardens display 7 million flowers each spring in stripes of every colour.

stories.question: Who was Anne Frank?
stories.body: During World War 2, when Nazi Germany occupied the Netherlands, a Jewish family — the Franks — hid in a secret apartment behind a movable bookcase above an Amsterdam warehouse, for over two years. Their teenage daughter Anne kept a diary describing daily life in hiding, her hopes, fears and frustrations as a young girl growing up locked away. They were betrayed in 1944 and sent to concentration camps. Anne died at age 15 in Bergen-Belsen, just weeks before the camp was liberated. Her father Otto survived, found her diary, and published it in 1947. It has now been translated into more than 70 languages and read by tens of millions of people. The hidden apartment is preserved as the Anne Frank House, one of the most-visited museums in the country.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Anne Frank wrote: "In spite of everything I still believe that people are really good at heart." What does it take for someone to believe that, in the situation she was in?

stories.question: Why was the Netherlands so rich in the 1600s?
stories.body: In the 1600s, the small Netherlands became the richest country in the world. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) was the first multinational corporation, with a fleet of ships dominating global trade. They imported spices from Indonesia, sugar from Brazil, cocoa from Africa, and silk from China. Amsterdam was the financial capital of the world. Artists like Rembrandt and Vermeer painted some of the greatest pictures ever made. But the Dutch also did terrible things during this "Golden Age": they were among the biggest slave traders in the Caribbean and ran plantations using enslaved people. The Netherlands only abolished slavery in 1863, three decades after Britain. The Dutch government has only in recent years officially apologised for this history.

convoQuestions.question: Best Dutch thing you have eaten so far?
How does it feel to be in a place where bikes always win against cars?
Have you noticed how tall and thin the canal houses are? Why do you think they are like that?
The Dutch are famously direct and say what they mean. Have you noticed it? Like it?
Have you seen a real windmill in action? Or a tulip field? Or a houseboat?
Most of the country shouldn't be there — the sea would take it back without the dykes. What do you do with a country built on top of water?

wordSearch.hint: Dutch foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Mallard duck
animals.description: On every canal in Amsterdam and every park pond in the country.

animals.name: Holstein-Friesian cow
animals.description: Black-and-white dairy cow in flat green fields between the cities.

animals.name: Mute swan
animals.description: Gliding on canals and waterways, sometimes hissy if you get too close.

animals.name: Texel sheep
animals.description: The plump white sheep on Texel and other Wadden Islands.

animals.name: Konik pony
animals.description: Semi-wild grey-dun ponies in the Oostvaardersplassen wetland nature reserve.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Reintroduced and now common again in summer in rural Holland.
